summary: Jeff Atwood shares his approach to diagnosing internet connection problems using two lightweight tools after experiencing bandwidth issues from a traffic spike. He frames internet troubleshooting around two key questions: bandwidth (how wide is your connection) and latency (how fast is your connection). He recommends NetMeter for monitoring bandwidth usage over time and PingPlotter for tracking latency and packet loss. The post emphasizes that while these aren't enterprise-grade tools, their simplicity makes them practical for gathering evidence to present to ISPs.
key_points: Internet connection quality has two distinct dimensions: bandwidth (throughput capacity) and latency (response time)
Consumer internet connections are highly asymmetric, with download speeds often 10x faster than upload speeds
Simple, lightweight monitoring tools can be more practical than complex enterprise solutions for basic troubleshooting
Collecting data over days or weeks provides much stronger evidence than anecdotal complaints when dealing with ISP support
Visual graphs of connection performance are more persuasive to tech support than vague reports of slowness
money_quotes: The two most important questions to ask are: How wide is your internet connection? How fast is your internet connection?
Your typical cable modem is around 300 kilobytes per second when downloading (sometimes even faster), but only 30 kilobytes per second when uploading.
Unless your connection suffers from severe packet loss, this is a bit less of a priority than raw bandwidth.
Now, these are intentionally lightweight tools. They're no match for fancy, dedicated networking diagnostic apps. But they're simple to use and easy to gather data with.
When you're calling your ISP's tech support, having these kinds of graphs to email them carries a heck of a lot more weight than generic complaints about 'slowness.'
key_insight: When troubleshooting internet issues, simple tools that collect data over time are more valuable than sophisticated one-time diagnostics because they provide concrete evidence for ISP support conversations.
